scenario: Apex Insurance authors a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision table that prices 8000 policies per week for new auto applicants. A junior modeler asks which sub-element of a rule carries the value the rule places in the evaluated decision result when the rule fires for a single applicant.
question: Which sub-element of a DMN decision table rule carries the conclusion value that the rule contributes to the result?

options.text: The outputEntry element holds the FEEL expression that becomes part of the decision result when the rule is satisfied
The inputEntry element holds the FEEL expression that becomes part of the decision result when the rule is satisfied
The hitPolicy attribute on the decisionTable holds the value the rule contributes to the decision result when satisfied
The label attribute on the rule element holds the value the rule contributes to the decision result when satisfied

a.text: Correct. The DMN docs describe the outputEntry as the conclusion side of a rule: when every input entry is satisfied, the decision result contains the outputEntry value from that rule.

b.text: Incorrect. The inputEntry element holds the condition side of the rule, not its conclusion; the produced value is carried by the outputEntry element instead.

c.text: Incorrect. The hitPolicy attribute governs how multiple satisfied rules are combined for the table, but it does not carry the per-rule output value itself.

d.text: Incorrect. The DMN docs do not describe a label attribute on the rule element as the carrier of the result value; the rule's value sits inside its outputEntry child.

explanation: A rule is built from input entries and output entries: input entries are the conditions and output entries are the conclusion expressions whose values are placed into the decision result when every condition is satisfied. The XML places both as children of the rule element.
